content.problemStatement:
Great! You have successfully built a **Log File Parser** using C++. 🎉

Your program can now **create, view, filter, search, and analyze logs** with ease through a simple menu-driven interface.

Here are some improvements you can try:


**Use a Database** – Replace the plain text file with a lightweight database like SQLite for structured storage and faster access.
**Enhanced Filtering** – Add support for combined filters (e.g., log level + keyword or date-based search).
**Log Export Feature** – Enable users to export filtered or complete logs to a CSV or JSON file for external use.
**Final Updates:**

Ensure all core functionalities (viewing, filtering, searching, statistics) work reliably.




Test edge cases such as:



Invalid or unsupported log levels
Empty or missing log files
Keywords that yield no results



Improve usability with clear messages, user prompts, and error handling.



Try implementing these improvements to make your C++ Log File Parser even more feature-rich and user-friendly! 🚀
constraints: LOG_LEVEL is one of DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
1. A line with an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000), the number of log entries.
2. N lines follow, each in the exact format "LOG_LEVEL KEYWORD: MESSAGE" where LOG_LEVEL ∈ {DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL}, KEYWORD contains no spaces, and MESSAGE may contain spaces.
3. A line with the filter criteria. The filter is either two tokens "LOG_LEVEL KEYWORD" (both must match) or a single token "LOG_LEVEL" (only the level must match). Tokens are separated by a single space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output every log entry that satisfies the filter, preserving the original order, each on its own line in the same format as the input. If no entry matches, output a single line "No logs found.". Print a blank line between consecutive test cases (i.e., after each test case except the last).

sampleTestCases.input:
2
3
INFO start: System started
ERROR disk: Disk failure
WARNING memory: Low memory
ERROR disk
2
DEBUG init: Initialization complete
INFO load: Load successful
INFO load
sampleTestCases.output:
ERROR disk: Disk failure

INFO load: Load successful
sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: filter "ERROR disk" matches only the second log entry. Test case 2: filter "INFO load" matches the second log entry. A blank line separates the outputs of the two test cases.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
4
DEBUG config: Config loaded
DEBUG start: Starting service
INFO start: Service started
ERROR crash: Unexpected crash
DEBUG
sampleTestCases.output:
DEBUG config: Config loaded
DEBUG start: Starting service
sampleTestCases.explanation: Filter contains only the level "DEBUG", so both logs whose level is DEBUG are printed in their original order.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
2
WARNING temp: Temperature high
CRITICAL overload: System overload
INFO status
sampleTestCases.explanation: The filter "INFO status" looks for level INFO and keyword status, but neither log matches, so the fallback message is printed.

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.input:
1
15
INFO init: System init
DEBUG load: Loading modules
ERROR disk: Disk error
WARNING mem: Memory low
ERROR net: Network failure
INFO user: User login
DEBUG config: Config set
ERROR cpu: CPU overload
INFO shutdown: System down
DEBUG ping: Ping test
WARNING temp: Temperature high
ERROR gpu: GPU failure
INFO restart: Restarting
DEBUG cleanup: Cleanup done
ERROR power: Power loss
ERROR
hidden.expectedOutput:
ERROR disk: Disk error
ERROR net: Network failure
ERROR cpu: CPU overload
ERROR gpu: GPU failure
ERROR power: Power loss

hidden.label: edge case
hidden.input:
1
3
INFO start: Begin
INFO stop: End
DEBUG start: Debugging
INFO pause

solution.approach: For each test case we store the N log lines. The filter line is split into tokens – the first token is always the required log level, the second token (if present) is the required keyword. Then we iterate over the stored logs in order. For each log we locate the first ':' character, take the substring before it, split it by whitespace to obtain the level and keyword. A log matches if its level equals the filter level and, when a filter keyword is provided, its keyword also equals the filter keyword. Matching logs are printed immediately (or collected). If no log matches we print "No logs found.". After processing a test case we output an extra blank line unless it is the last test case. The algorithm is O(N) per test case and uses O(N) memory to store the logs.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T · N) where N is the number of logs in each test case (linear scan).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case to store the log strings.
